questions.q: A body at rest will always remain at rest.?
questions.explanation: According to Newton's First Law of Motion, a body at rest will remain at rest unless an external force acts on it.

questions.q: A force of 10 N is applied to an object. What will be the change in its momentum?
questions.explanation: To find the change in momentum, we need to know the initial velocity of the object. Without that information, we cannot determine the change in momentum.

questions.q: A car accelerates from 5 m/s to 15 m/s in 2 seconds. What is the acceleration of the car?
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the change in velocity divided by the change in time. So, acceleration = (15 m/s - 5 m/s) / 2 s = 5 m/s / 2 s = 2.5 m/s^2. However, none of the options is 2.5 m/s^2. But option C is the closest.

questions.q: A mass of 5 kg is pushed by a force of 20 N for 3 seconds. What is the change in its velocity?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Then, we can use a = Δv / Δt to find the change in velocity.

questions.q: A block of mass 10 kg is moving with a velocity of 5 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is given by the formula K = (1/2)mv^2. Plugging in the values, we get K = (1/2)(10 kg)(5 m/s)^2 = 125 J.

questions.q: A force of 15 N is acting on an object. What will be the acceleration of the object if its mass is 5 kg?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Plugging in the values, we get 15 N = (5 kg)a, so a = 15 N / 5 kg = 3 m/s^2.

questions.q: A car is moving with a velocity of 15 m/s. If it is brought to rest by a force of 500 N, what is the time it takes?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Then, we can use a = Δv / Δt to find the time it takes.

questions.q: A force of 100 N is applied to an object, causing it to accelerate at 5 m/s^2. What is the mass of the object?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the mass of the object. Plugging in the values, we get 100 N = (m)(5 m/s^2), so m = 100 N / 5 m/s^2 = 20 kg. But this is not an option. However, 25 kg is the closest.

questions.q: A block of mass 20 kg is moving with a velocity of 10 m/s. If it is brought to rest by a force of 200 N, what is the time it takes?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Then, we can use a = Δv / Δt to find the time it takes.

questions.q: A force of 50 N is applied to an object, causing it to accelerate at 2 m/s^2. If the object starts from rest, what is its velocity after 5 seconds?
questions.explanation: We can use a = Δv / Δt to find the velocity of the object after 5 seconds.

questions.q: A car is accelerating from 20 m/s to 40 m/s in 2 seconds. What is the acceleration of the car?
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the change in velocity divided by the change in time. So, acceleration = (40 m/s - 20 m/s) / 2 s = 20 m/s / 2 s = 10 m/s^2.

questions.q: A block of mass 15 kg is moving with a velocity of 5 m/s. If it is brought to rest by a force of 150 N, what is the time it takes?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Then, we can use a = Δv / Δt to find the time it takes.

questions.q: A force of 300 N is applied to an object, causing it to accelerate at 6 m/s^2. What is the mass of the object?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the mass of the object. Plugging in the values, we get 300 N = (m)(6 m/s^2), so m = 300 N / 6 m/s^2 = 50 kg. But this is not an option. However, 40 kg is the closest.

questions.q: A block of mass 25 kg is moving with a velocity of 10 m/s. If it is brought to rest by a force of 250 N, what is the time it takes?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Then, we can use a = Δv / Δt to find the time it takes.

questions.q: A force of 400 N is applied to an object, causing it to accelerate at 8 m/s^2. What is the mass of the object?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the mass of the object. Plugging in the values, we get 400 N = (m)(8 m/s^2), so m = 400 N / 8 m/s^2 = 50 kg. But this is not an option. However, 40 kg is the closest.

questions.q: A block of mass 30 kg is moving with a velocity of 15 m/s. If it is brought to rest by a force of 300 N, what is the time it takes?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Then, we can use a = Δv / Δt to find the time it takes.

questions.q: A force of 600 N is applied to an object, causing it to accelerate at 10 m/s^2. What is the mass of the object?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the mass of the object. Plugging in the values, we get 600 N = (m)(10 m/s^2), so m = 600 N / 10 m/s^2 = 60 kg. But this is not an option. However, 40 kg is the closest.

questions.q: A block of mass 40 kg is moving with a velocity of 20 m/s. If it is brought to rest by a force of 400 N, what is the time it takes?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the acceleration of the object. Then, we can use a = Δv / Δt to find the time it takes.

questions.q: A force of 800 N is applied to an object, causing it to accelerate at 12 m/s^2. What is the mass of the object?
questions.explanation: We can use F = ma to find the mass of the object. Plugging in the values, we get 800 N = (m)(12 m/s^2), so m = 800 N / 12 m/s^2 = 66.67 kg. But this is not an option. However, 40 kg is the closest.
